sources: DGFT Foreign Trade Policy 2023 (FTP 2023), effective April 1, 2023
DGFT SCOMET Notification No. 27/2023, dated August 28, 2023

regulations.title: IEC Mandatory for All Exports
regulations.text: No export or import shall be made by any person without obtaining an Importer Exporter Code (IEC) unless specifically exempted. The IEC is a 10-digit code issued by DGFT and must be quoted on all shipping bills, bills of export, and export declarations. Non-quoting or incorrect IEC on export documents is grounds for rejection by customs and cancellation of export benefits.

regulations.title: Annual IEC Updation Mandatory
regulations.citation: DGFT FTP 2023, Chapter 2, Para 2.07; DGFT Trade Notice 10/2021
regulations.text: It is mandatory for IEC holders to electronically update their IEC profile annually between April and June each year, even if there are no changes. Failure to update IEC will result in deactivation of the IEC. Exports under a deactivated IEC are treated as exports without IEC, attracting penalties under Customs Act 1962.

regulations.title: RCMC Required for FTP Scheme Benefits
regulations.text: Registration-cum-Membership Certificate (RCMC) issued by the relevant Export Promotion Council (EPC) or Commodity Board is mandatory for exporters seeking any authorisation, licence, or benefit under FTP 2023 — including Advance Authorisation, EPCG, and status holder benefits. Note: RCMC is NOT required for post-export remission schemes (RoDTEP, RoSCTL, Duty Drawback). The RCMC must state the export product group and be valid at the time of shipment.

regulations.title: Export Authorisation Required for Pharma Products to Controlled Destinations
regulations.citation: DGFT FTP 2023, Chapter 4, Para 4.42; Schedule-2 ITC(HS)
regulations.text: Export of pharmaceutical products classified under HS Chapter 29 (Organic Chemicals), Chapter 30 (Pharmaceutical Products), and certain chemicals under Chapter 28 to specific countries requires prior export authorisation from DGFT. Additionally, exporters of pharmaceutical products must be registered with the Pharmaceuticals Export Promotion Council (Pharmexcil) and obtain RCMC. Consignments exported without valid authorisation where required are liable to be detained at port.

regulations.title: SCOMET Export Authorisation — Toxic Chemicals (Category 1)
regulations.citation: DGFT SCOMET Notification No. 27/2023, dated August 28, 2023; Appendix 3 to Schedule-2 ITC(HS), Category 1
regulations.text: Export of items listed under SCOMET Category 1 (Toxic Chemical Agents and other chemicals including those listed under the Chemical Weapons Convention) requires prior authorisation from DGFT. These include sulphur mustard, lewisite, nitrogen mustard, organophosphate compounds, and precursors listed in CWC Schedules 1, 2, and 3. Export without valid SCOMET authorisation is a criminal offence under the WMD Act 2005 and the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992.

regulations.title: SCOMET Export Authorisation — Advanced Materials (Category 3)
regulations.citation: DGFT SCOMET Notification No. 27/2023, August 28, 2023; Appendix 3, Category 3
regulations.text: Category 3 of the SCOMET list covers Materials, Materials Processing Equipment and related technologies including: maraging steel with tensile strength exceeding 2050 MPa, zirconium and zirconium alloys (where Hf content is less than 1:500), beryllium metal and alloys, magnesium alloys in specific particle sizes, and carbon fibre with tensile strength exceeding 2000 MPa. Export of these materials requires a valid SCOMET authorisation from DGFT. End-user certificates (EUC) from the importing country government are mandatory.

regulations.title: SCOMET Export Authorisation — Electronics and Computers (Category 7)
regulations.citation: DGFT SCOMET Notification No. 27/2023, August 28, 2023; Appendix 3, Category 7
regulations.text: Category 7 of SCOMET covers Electronics and Computers with controlled specifications: (a) oscilloscopes with sampling rates above 1 GS/s, (b) signal analysers with bandwidths above 31.8 GHz, (c) computers with certain aggregate data rates, (d) microprocessors with specific performance thresholds. Exports to non-allied countries require SCOMET authorisation. For electronics exports to all destinations, the shipper must verify item specifications against the SCOMET list before shipment.

regulations.title: Shipping Bill Mandatory for Export by Sea/Air
regulations.citation: Customs Act, 1962, Section 50; Shipping Bill (Electronic Declaration) Regulations, 2011
regulations.text: As per Section 50 of the Customs Act, 1962, the exporter of any goods (by vessel or aircraft) shall present a Shipping Bill electronically to the proper officer. The Shipping Bill must contain: exporter name and IEC, consignee name and address, detailed description of goods, HS code (at 8-digit ITC-HS level), quantity and unit of measurement, FOB value in INR and foreign currency, port of loading, and country of destination. Any mismatch between the Shipping Bill and the supporting documents (invoice, packing list) is treated as mis-declaration under Section 111(m).

regulations.title: Penalty for Mis-declaration of Goods — Weight, Description, Value
regulations.text: Under Section 111(m) of the Customs Act, 1962, any goods that are mis-declared in respect of their quantity, weight, description, origin, or value are liable to confiscation. Section 114AA provides that any person who makes, signs, or uses any declaration or document knowing it to be false or incorrect in any material particular shall be liable to a penalty not exceeding five times the value of the goods. A weight difference between the shipping bill and the packing list/invoice constitutes mis-declaration. Even unintentional mis-declarations do not free the exporter from liability — the Customs House Agent (CHA) error does not absolve the exporter.

regulations.title: Confiscation of Goods for Export in Violation of Restrictions
regulations.text: Under Section 113 of the Customs Act, 1962, goods attempted to be exported — (d) which are prohibited goods, or (e) which do not correspond in respect of value or any other particular with the entry made under the Act — shall be liable to confiscation. This applies to goods on the Negative List of exports, goods requiring export authorisation that has not been obtained, and goods where the declared description, HS code, or value is incorrect. Exporters must ensure the physical goods match the invoice, packing list, and shipping bill in every material particular.

regulations.title: RCMC from AEPC Required for Garment/Textile Exporters
regulations.citation: DGFT FTP 2023, Chapter 2, Para 2.12; AEPC Registration Requirements
regulations.text: Exporters of readymade garments and made-up textile articles (HS Chapters 61, 62, 63) who wish to avail benefits under FTP 2023 (Advance Authorisation, EPCG, etc.) must obtain RCMC from the Apparel Export Promotion Council (AEPC). The RCMC must match the product group being exported. Exporters who change their product mix must update their RCMC accordingly. Garment samples exported without commercial value for trade fairs are exempt from this requirement.

regulations.title: RCMC from EPCH Required for Handicraft Exporters
regulations.citation: DGFT FTP 2023, Chapter 2, Para 2.12; EPCH Registration
regulations.text: Exporters of handicrafts (including wooden articles, brass items, pottery, glassware, carpets, gems and jewellery) who wish to avail FTP 2023 benefits must obtain RCMC from the Export Promotion Council for Handicrafts (EPCH). Handicraft exporters in Towns of Export Excellence (TEE) designated under FTP 2023 — including Moradabad (brass/metalware) and Varanasi (handloom/handicraft) — are entitled to additional benefits. Proper country-of-origin marking is mandatory for handicraft exports.

regulations.title: FSSAI NOC / Registration Required for Food Exports
regulations.citation: Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006; FSSAI Export Regulations; CBIC Circular 2022
regulations.text: Exporters of food products (including spices, processed foods, dairy, beverages, organic products) must obtain FSSAI Central License or FSSAI Registration. For exports, an FSSAI No-Objection Certificate (NOC) is required at the port of export. The shipping documents (invoice, packing list) must carry the FSSAI licence number. Export of food products without valid FSSAI licence is subject to seizure at port under Food Safety and Standards Act 2006. Products must comply with the importing country's standards and carry appropriate labels.

regulations.title: BIS Certification Mandatory for Quality-Controlled Goods
regulations.citation: Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 2016; BIS (Compulsory Registration) Order; Export Inspection Council (EIC)
regulations.text: Certain goods are covered under the BIS Compulsory Certification Scheme and cannot be exported without a valid BIS licence. These include electronic goods, electrical equipment, steel products, cement, and packaged drinking water. For exports, the Export Inspection Council (EIC) under DGFT may mandate pre-shipment inspection and certification for specific product categories. The BIS ISI mark must appear on relevant goods and packing. Export of non-conforming goods may attract penalty under Customs Act 1962 Section 113.

regulations.title: Correct 8-Digit ITC-HS Code Mandatory on All Export Documents
regulations.citation: Customs Act 1962, Section 50; ITC (HS) Classification of Export and Import Items 2018; CBIC Circular 18/2019
regulations.text: All export declarations (shipping bill, invoice, packing list) must carry the correct 8-digit ITC-HS code (harmonized system code). Using an incorrect HS code — even if the 6-digit base code is correct — is treated as mis-declaration under Section 111(m) of the Customs Act 1962 and can attract penalties up to 5x the value of goods under Section 114AA. The HS code must be identical across all three documents: commercial invoice, packing list, and shipping bill. Discrepancies are flagged by ICEGATE Risk Management System and trigger examination orders.

regulations.title: Export Proceeds Realisation within 9 Months (FEMA)
regulations.citation: Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999; RBI Master Direction on Export of Goods and Services; RBI/ 2015-16/12 FED Master Direction
regulations.text: Under FEMA 1999 and RBI regulations, export proceeds must be realised and repatriated to India within 9 months from the date of export for all exporters (exports to warehouses outside India have a 15-month period). SEZ units have a 12-month period. Failure to realise export proceeds within the specified period is a violation of FEMA and may attract penalty. The AD Bank (Authorised Dealer) must be informed of all export transactions. Export documents must include the AD Code registered with customs at the port of export.

regulations.title: CITES Permit Required for Wildlife and Wild Plant Exports
regulations.citation: Wildlife Protection Act, 1972; CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species); DGFT FTP 2023 Appendix 3
regulations.text: Export of any animal, bird, reptile or their derivatives, and listed plant species covered under CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species) is prohibited or strictly regulated. A CITES export permit issued by the Chief Wildlife Warden of the concerned state is mandatory before customs clearance. Even artificially propagated plant species listed under CITES Appendix II require a phytosanitary certificate and CITES permit. Export without a valid CITES permit is a criminal offence under the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.

regulations.title: Country of Origin Declaration Mandatory on Export Documents
regulations.citation: Customs Act 1962, Section 50; Rules of Origin (Certificate of Origin) Regulations; DGFT FTP 2023 Chapter 2
regulations.text: All export documents must correctly declare the country of origin as India. For preferential trade agreements (FTAs with ASEAN, UAE, UK, Australia, etc.), a Certificate of Origin (CoO) from the respective authority (e.g., Export Inspection Council, FIEO, chambers of commerce) is required to avail preferential duties. The origin declaration must comply with applicable Rules of Origin (substantial transformation criteria). False origin declaration is an offence under Section 111(m) of the Customs Act.
